subtitle: Telehealth · AI-Augmented Delivery · Engineering + Clinical + Ops
hero_title:
I don't color-code Jira
and call it a day.
hero_sub: I ship using Claude Code. I automate recurring PM tasks. I write specs that AI coding agents execute. I surface blockers before they become incidents and escalate with proposed solutions. This is how I work — not how I aspire to work.

the_case.need: Hands-on AI tooling use — automated your own work, built a workflow, or written prompts that replaced a recurring task
the_case.answer: Jira AI and Copilot embedded in enterprise release governance at Crocs (~30% overhead reduction). Six production applications shipped this year using Claude Code. Prompt engineering and agent-executable spec writing across multiple engagements.
the_case.detail: At Crocs I embedded Jira AI into backlog management, sprint summaries, and stakeholder documentation — compressing multi-hour recurring tasks into minutes. I used Microsoft Copilot for async communication drafts that went directly to leadership. At the same time I shipped six production applications using Claude Code: audio DSP, real-time 3D, game systems, a React/TypeScript platformer with live Gemini API integration, and a secure Next.js portfolio. The AI tools are not something I plan to use — they are how the work gets done.

the_case.need: Write specs and project briefs that AI agents and engineers can execute with minimal back-and-forth
the_case.answer: Every production application this year started with a spec I wrote for Claude Code to execute. Every sprint at Eptura and Crocs had task breakdowns structured for autonomous execution. This is the core of how I work.
the_case.detail: Writing specs that are executable — by engineers, by AI agents, or by junior team members without synchronous clarification — requires precision, structure, and understanding of what information an executor actually needs. I have been writing these specs for ten years. The difference now is that the executor is sometimes an AI coding agent, which has made my spec quality noticeably better because AI agents surface every ambiguity immediately. At Understood.org I wrote specs that a globally distributed team (12+ countries) executed asynchronously across time zones — the same discipline, the same stakes.

the_case.need: Own cross-functional delivery across Engineering, Product, Clinical, and Ops
the_case.answer: Eptura: owned delivery across ~10 distributed teams spanning Engineering, Product, UX, Data, and Marketing. Crocs: held the seam between Engineering, QA, Change Management, and Business under a hard deadline. Understood.org: Engineering, QA, Content, and Compliance in a regulated environment.
the_case.detail: Holding the cross-functional seam is not a metaphor — it is a structural role. At Eptura I was the organizational connective tissue that maintained alignment across teams with competing priorities and different operating norms. At Crocs I translated compliance requirements into engineering tasks and engineering constraints into executive updates — without losing fidelity in either direction. At Understood.org I held the thread between global engineering teams, healthcare-adjacent compliance requirements, and executive stakeholders who needed clear answers on a program that was at risk.

the_case.need: Use AI to remove work — automate recurring status updates, meeting notes, reporting
the_case.answer: Jira AI: automated sprint summaries, backlog health reports, and retrospective theme synthesis at Crocs. Microsoft Copilot: automated stakeholder communication drafts and meeting documentation. Claude: automated spec generation and delivery analysis across multiple engagements.
the_case.detail: The framing I use for AI in PM work is: what is the recurring task that takes 2 hours and produces something a decision-maker reads in 2 minutes? Those are the tasks I automate first. At Crocs I automated the weekly release readiness report — Jira AI pulled the data, I reviewed the output, stakeholders received it. The output was better than what I would have written manually because I had time to review rather than generate. The ~30% overhead reduction at Crocs was not from one automation — it was from systematically identifying and replacing every recurring low-value task.

the_case.answer: Understood.org (2019–2020): regulated, healthcare-adjacent delivery environment. Privacy and confidentiality standards enforced throughout. WCAG 2.1 AA compliance as core Definition of Done. Global distribution with data handling requirements.
the_case.detail: At Understood.org I delivered a platform serving users with learning and attention differences — a healthcare-adjacent, regulated context with real compliance accountability. I enforced privacy and confidentiality standards in the delivery process, embedded WCAG 2.1 AA as a non-negotiable Definition of Done requirement, and navigated the same tension LumiMeds faces: moving fast while maintaining compliance. I did not have to learn that tension from scratch — I have operated inside it.

the_case.need: High agency — identify problems, propose solutions, execute without waiting to be managed
the_case.answer: Founded OVRSTCH and Transelectronic Labs — no manager, no direction, no waiting. Shipped six production applications this year independently. Took over a distressed program at Understood.org and delivered it. The agency is not an aspiration; it is the operating mode.
the_case.detail: High agency is demonstrated, not claimed. I founded two companies. I shipped six production applications this year across domains I had not previously worked in (audio DSP, real-time 3D graphics, game physics, AI integration) because I identified a problem (I wanted to build things) and executed on it without external direction. At Understood.org I was handed a distressed program with 12 weeks left, a globally distributed team, and no established rhythm — and I built one. Agency under pressure, with real stakes, is different from agency when conditions are favorable.

nodes.label: Claude Code
nodes.desc: AI coding agent used across six production applications — architecture, implementation, debugging, and spec execution
nodes.pm_role: Write executable specs that Claude Code implements. Review and validate output. Ship. This is the workflow — not a pilot program.

nodes.label: Jira & Jira AI
nodes.desc: Primary delivery tracking with AI-assisted backlog synthesis, sprint summaries, and reporting automation
nodes.pm_role: Embedded at Crocs for sprint documentation and backlog management. ~30% overhead reduction. The output got sharper because I had time to review instead of generate.

nodes.label: GitHub & GitHub Actions
nodes.desc: Version control and CI/CD pipelines — six production applications deployed via GitHub Actions
nodes.pm_role: Hands-on CI/CD experience. Can read a PR, understand a deployment pipeline, and hold my own in an engineering infrastructure conversation.

nodes.label: Microsoft Copilot
nodes.desc: AI-assisted async stakeholder communication, meeting documentation, and reporting
nodes.pm_role: Automated recurring stakeholder communication drafts and meeting synthesis at Crocs. Frees capacity from administrative output to actual delivery work.

nodes.label: Azure DevOps (ADO) / Asana / Jira
nodes.desc: Project tracking, dependency management, sprint boards, delivery dashboards, risk registers
nodes.pm_role: ADO configuration owner at Eptura PMO (~10 teams). Deep Jira administration. Fluent in Linear, Asana, Notion equivalents — the tool is a minor variable.

when_things_break.scenario: Scope creep is eating the sprint — engineering is building things product mentioned once in a Slack thread
response: Stop the work before the next standup, not after the sprint review
Pull the original initiative brief and the current task list side by side — show the delta explicitly
Facilitate a 30-minute scope triage with Product and Engineering leads: what is committed, what is new, who approved the change?
Add a scope change log to the project documentation — every change gets a decision owner and a date
Version-control the sprint goal so 'what we agreed to' is not a memory exercise

when_things_break.scenario: Clinical and Engineering are misaligned on a compliance requirement mid-sprint
response: Stop the build that is at risk — the cost of shipping non-compliant is higher than the cost of pausing
Get the specific clinical requirement in writing before any engineering decision is made
Translate the compliance constraint into engineering language — what exactly needs to change and why
Escalate to leadership with a concrete decision request: do we accept the delay, de-scope, or accept the risk?
Document the decision and who made it before work resumes

when_things_break.scenario: A recurring status update is taking 3 hours every week and nobody reads it
response: Stop writing it — send a 3-sentence version and see if anyone notices
If nobody notices, the 3-sentence version is the new format
If someone does notice, find out what they actually need and build that instead
Automate the data collection using Jira AI or ADO reporting so the output generates itself
Version-control the format so it can be improved without losing institutional memory

how_i_run_a_room: Every meeting has a stated decision or output — if it doesn't, it doesn't happen
Status updates are async, written, and structured for skimming — not oral reports
AI tools compress the administrative side of delivery so the human time goes to judgment calls
Specs are written before implementation starts — not after the engineer asks what they're building
Risk registers are live documents, not quarterly deliverables
Blockers surface in standups with proposed solutions, not just problem statements
Every initiative has a baseline, a target, and a definition of done before the first task is assigned

points: Six production applications shipped using Claude Code — audio DSP, 3D graphics, game systems, AI integration
Wrote specs that Claude Code executed; reviewed, tested, and shipped the output
Full CI/CD via GitHub Actions; all publicly live on GitHub Pages
Proved the workflow: spec → agent execution → human review → production
Owned cross-functional delivery across Engineering, QA, Change Management, and Business
Jira AI + Copilot automated recurring PM tasks — ~30% overhead reduction
Risk register and Release Radar tracking framework built and maintained
On-time go-live with clean post-launch stabilization under hard deadline pressure

track_record.role: Senior PM / Agile Coach — Enterprise PMO
points: ~10 distributed teams — held the cross-functional seam across Engineering, Product, UX, Data, Marketing
Audited and killed unnecessary rituals — teams shipped more because they met less
35%+ delivery efficiency gain; 20% sprint carryover reduction
Built ADO/Jira/Smartsheet dashboards — every initiative had a baseline, target, and definition of done
HIPAA-adjacent workflow constraints; privacy and confidentiality standards enforced throughout
Wrote specs executed by a globally distributed team (12+ countries) without synchronous clarification
On-time launch, no critical regressions — distressed program, final crucial months
WCAG 2.1 AA compliance as hard Definition of Done requirement

before: AI tools are in the roadmap but nobody has automated their own workflow yet
Specs are written after engineers ask what they're building — the back-and-forth is the bottleneck
Status updates take 3 hours to write and nobody reads them
Blockers surface at retrospectives instead of standups — too late to protect the sprint
after: Recurring PM admin is automated — status updates generate themselves, meeting notes are structured and searchable
Specs are written before implementation starts — engineers and AI agents execute with minimal clarification
Every initiative has a baseline, a target, and a definition of done before the first task is assigned
Blockers surface in standups with proposed solutions — not just problem statements that wait for someone else to solve

the_ramp.deliverable: Map the full initiative landscape. Understand what is in flight, what is blocked, and what definition of done looks like for each. Identify the first three things that need a spec and don't have one.
Own one active initiative end to end. Write the first spec structured for agent or engineer execution. Identify the first recurring PM task to automate.
First automation live. Delivery dashboard publishing reliably. Clinical/Engineering/Ops seam owned and documented. First risk escalated with a proposed solution.

Cross-functional delivery running without over-communication. AI tools embedded in at least three recurring PM workflows. Team can see whether an initiative shipped successfully — because the spec was complete.
